payload.subject: theortical computer science
payload.custom_instruction:
Generate a question paper with the following structure. Total marks = 50.

Section A (20 marks):
Q1.A (10 marks) — Generate 2 sub-questions (5 marks each). Label as 1. and 2.
OR
Q1.B (10 marks) — Generate 2 alternative sub-questions (5 marks each). Label as 1. and 2.

Section B (20 marks):
Q2.A (10 marks) OR Q2.B (10 marks) — Each as a single long answer question
Q3.A (10 marks) OR Q3.B (10 marks) — Each as a single long answer question

Section C (10 marks):
Q4.A (10 marks) OR Q4.B (10 marks) — Each as a single long answer question
Q5.A (10 marks) OR Q5.B (10 marks) — Each as a single long answer question

Rules:
- Every question must have an A and B alternative
- A and B in each pair must be based on the same topic/CO
- Label alternatives clearly as Q1.A / Q1.B etc.
- Total marks must equal exactly 50
payload.fileContent:
Course Outcomes:
- On successful completion, of course, learner/student will be able to:
- 1 Recall definitions of formal languages, automata, and expressions. (remembering)
- 2 Explain equivalences and conversions between finite automata models. (understanding)
- 3 Construct regular expressions, finite automata, and regular grammars and apply Pumping
- Lemma and closure properties. (applying)
- 4 Analyze context-free grammars, derivations, ambiguity. (analyzing)
- 5 Construct and evaluate pushdown automata and use instantaneous descriptions to determine
- string acceptance. (evaluating)
- 6 Design Turing machines for language recognition/computation and explain undecidability.
- (creating)

Detailed Syllabus Units:
Module/Unit: Module Detailed Contents Hours
  • 1 Foundations of Computation 3
  • Strings, Alphabet, Language, Operations, Finite state machine,
  • definitions, finite automaton model, acceptance of strings, and
  • languages.
  • Self Study: Solve problems as a finite automaton.
  • 2 Finite Automata 10
  • Finite Automata: DFA and NFA, and Equivalence of DFA & NFA,
  • Minimization of DFA, Mealy and Moore machines and
  • equivalence.
  • Self Learning: Explore how Mealy and Moore machines are used in
  • real-world digital systems like traffic lights, elevators, and vending
  • machines.
  • 3 Regular Languages 9
  • Regular Grammar, Closure Properties, Arden Theorem, Regular
  • expression to FA, DFA to Regular expression.
  • Pumping Lemma, Decision Properties of Regular Languages.
  • Self Learning: Practical Applications of Regular Languages,
  • 4 Context-Free Grammars 9
  • Production systems, Right linear grammar and Left Linear Grammar,
  • Equivalence of grammar and finite automata.
  • Context free grammars, Derivations, Ambiguity, Ambiguous to
  • Unambiguous CFG.
  • Normal forms, Pumping Lemma for CFLs.
  • Self Learning: Subfamilies of CFL
  • 5 Pushdown Automata 6
  • Pushdown Automata (PDA), Language Acceptance, Acceptance by
  • final state and empty stack
  • Equivalence between push- down automata and context-free
  • grammars.
  • Self Learning: Instantaneous descriptions (IDs) to follow PDA
  • computations.
  • 6 Turing Machines and Undecidability 8
  • Recursive & Recursively Enumerable Languages, Turing Machines:
  • Basic Model,Techniques for Turing machine construction,
  • Language Recognition.
  • Variants of TM, Universal Turing machine
  • Halting Problem, Decidable and undecidable problems, Post's
  • correspondence problem, Introduction to Theory of NP-
  • completeness.
  • Self Learning: Non- deterministic Turing machine, Rice's theorem
  • TOTAL 45

debug.section_requirements:
Section A: Objective Analysis | 10 questions | 1 marks each | 10 total marks
Section B: Conceptual Synthesis | 5 questions | 3 marks each | 15 total marks
Section C: Comprehensive Argumentation | 3 questions | 5 marks each | 15 total marks
IMPORTANT: Generate the COMPLETE paper for all sections above. Do not stop early, do not omit any section, and ensure the full paper covers 40 marks across the configured sections.
